segments.s602-g41: گر is the conditional particle 'if'. این اسپ is the subject phrase 'this steed' (این as demonstrative + اسپ an archaic form of اسب). سرگین و آب are the direct objects placed before the verb in classical/Persian verb‑final order, and افگند (classical past/pretended subjunctive of افکند) expresses the hypothetical action 'would cast/throw'. The whole clause reads 'if this steed were to throw dung and water'.
segments.s602-g42: وگر is a continuative conditional 'and if' / 'but if', introducing an alternative hypothetical. خشت این خانه را is the object phrase 'the brick of this house' with را marking definiteness/direct object; note the demonstrative این modifies خانه and the whole noun phrase precedes the verb. بشکند is a 3rd‑person potential/subjunctive form 'should break / were to break' agreeing with the implied agent (the steed).
segments.s602-g43: به شبگیر is a time phrase 'toward night / at dusk' (به + شبگیر). سرگینش is the possessed noun 'its dung' (سرگین + possessive -ش). بیرون کنی is the verb phrase 'you put/bring out' with بیرون (out) preceding the verb and کنی the 2nd‑person present/subjunctive form 'you should do' in classical style; together: 'you should take its dung out at night'.
segments.s602-g44: بروبی is from روبیدن (to sweep/clean) in the 2nd‑person form, an instruction 'sweep it away / make it tidy'. و links the two actions. خاکش 'its dirt/dust' is the object, and به هامون کنی uses به + هامون 'toward the plain' plus کنی (again 'you should do') meaning 'you should carry/throw its dust into the plain'.
segments.s602-g45: همان 'that very / the same' specifies the noun خشت (mudbrick). را marks خشت as the definite/direct object. نیز 'also' adds that this payment is in addition to prior obligations. تاوان دهی is a 2nd‑person verbal construction 'you shall give/pay compensation' (دهی here = 'you give' used as obligation/future in classical narrative).
segments.s602-g46: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بیدار گردی is a periphrastic expression 'you become awake / you wake' (بیدار 'awake' + گردی, an auxiliary/verb form meaning 'become'), so 'when you wake'. ز خواب 'from sleep' gives the source, and آن دهی repeats the verb 'you give it then' — آن (that/it) referring back to the payment and دهی 'you give'.
segments.s602-g47: بدو 'to him' is the prepositional phrase marking the indirect object/recipient. گفت is the reported‑speech verb 'said' (here listed senses do not include the past‑tense 'said', so the precise sense index is not available). بهرام is the speaker's name. پیمان کنم is the subordinate clause 'I will make a pact' with کنم (1st‑person present/subjunctive used for a future/intentional action). The word order is classical: to‑him said Bahram, I‑will‑make‑pact.
segments.s602-g48: برین is the fused form of بر + این meaning 'for/on these', so برین رنجها = 'for these hardships' (رنجها 'the sufferings/hardships'). سر گروگان کنم is an idiomatic construction 'I will make my head a hostage' or 'I will put my head up as pledge' — سر (head) + گروگان (hostage/pledge) made into a verb phrase with کنم 'I will do/make'. Here کنم is 1st‑person volitional/future.
segments.s602-g57: به گیتی is an adverbial phrase placed up front: به (preposition) + گیتی 'the world' = 'in/within the world'. هرانکس که دارد خورد is a relative clause 'whoever ...' (هرانکس که = 'whoever that'). دارد functions here as an auxiliary (literary usage) with خورد (a past participle/verb meaning 'befall; happen') to give the sense 'is afflicted' / 'has befallen' – literally 'whoever has befallen'. The clause sets the subject for the following admonition.
segments.s602-g58: سوی مردم بی‌نوا is a prepositional phrase 'toward the people who are needy' (سوی = 'toward', مردم = 'people', بی‌نوا = 'the poor/destitute'). ننگرد is a literary negative jussive/subjunctive ('let him not look' / 'should not regard'); here it expresses the moral command 'do not look down upon/regard with contempt'.
segments.s602-g59: بدو گفت بهرام is inverted word order typical of epic poetry: بدو ('to him') + گفت (here a verbal form 'said') + بهرام (the speaker's name) = 'to him Bahram said'. کاین داستان is the clause-head for what follows: کاین = 'that this/this that' and داستان = 'tale/story'.
segments.s602-g60: شنیدستم is an archaic past first-person form 'I heard / I was told'. از introduces the source 'from', گفته functions as 'saying/report' (i.e. 'report/accounts'), and باستان modifies گفته = 'ancient'. Together: 'I heard it from ancient reports.'
segments.s602-g61: شنیدم به گفتار uses شنیدم 'I heard' plus the adverbial phrase به گفتار ('by/through speech; by report'); و is the simple conjunction 'and'; دیدم کنون is 'I see now' (دیدم is past/presentive 'I have seen' or 'I see' in narrative usage, کنون = 'now'). The clause links what was heard to what is now perceived.
segments.s602-g62: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that ...'. برخواندی is a classical perfect/past form 'you have read aloud / you read' (literary) or generally 'was read aloud' depending on context; here it functions as the verb of the subordinate clause. از + گفته رهنمون = 'from the guiding/report' (گفته = 'report/account', رهنمون = 'guide/guidance'), so the clause says 'that (it) was read from the guiding report.'
segments.s602-g63: می‌آورد is a narrative-present verb here meaning 'he relates/relates (this)'. چون introduces the temporal clause 'when' (classical use), and خورده شد is a passive construction: خورده (past participle 'eaten') + شد (auxiliary 'was') = 'was eaten'. نان جهود = 'the bread of the Jews' (جهود is an archaic plural for Jews). The whole segment: 'he relates that when the Jews' bread was eaten...'.
segments.s602-g64: ازان = 'from that / thereupon' (refers back to the preceding event). می‌ورا is an archaic/obscure verb form in the manuscript (rare in classical Persian); here it participates in the resulting-clause construction together with شادمانی فزود, where شادمانی = 'joy' and فزود is the past 'increased / grew'. The net sense: 'from that, joy/rejoicing increased.'
segments.s602-g89: بدو گفت is a flattened sequence meaning “he said to him” (بدو = ‘to him’ + گفت = ‘said’). من کس ندارم is a negative possession clause: من = “I”, کس ندارم = “I have no one”. که introduces a relative clause modifying کس, and خاک is the direct object (the thing to be taken away). Word order is subject (من) + object-like phrase (کس ندارم) with the relative clause attached.
segments.s602-g90: This is a sequence of three verbs describing the desired action: بروبد (older/poetic infinitival/agentive sense ‘to pick up/remove’), برد (‘to take/carry’), ریزد (‘to pour/let fall’) followed by اندر مغاک ‘into the abyss’ (اندر = “into/in”, مغاک = “abyss”). The verbs are coordinated serially to express successive parts of the action.
segments.s602-g91: تو پیمان که کردی is ‘you made a covenant’ (تو = you, پیمان = covenant, کردی = you did/made). به کژی مبر is an imperative prohibition: به = ‘to/toward’, کژی = ‘crookedness/wrongdoing’, مبر = negative imperative ‘do not take/commit’. The clause links the promise (پیمان) to the injunction against wrongdoing.
segments.s602-g92: نباید که is an impersonal modal ‘it must not / one should not’. خوانمت is a first-person singular present/future-laden verb with enclitic object ‘-ت’: خوانم + -ت = ‘I call you / I will call you’. بیدادگر is the predicate noun ‘tyrant/unjust one’. Together: ‘one should not call you a tyrant.’
segments.s602-g93: چو بشنید = ‘when he heard’ (چو = when, بشنید = he heard). بهرام is the subject, and ازو این سخن means ‘this speech from him’ (ازو = from him, این سخن = this speech). Word order is typical classical Persian: temporal conjunction + verb + subject + source + object.
segments.s602-g94: یکی تازه اندیشه افگند بن is a compact poetic clause: یکی تازه اندیشه = ‘a fresh/new thought’, افگند = ‘he threw/cast/placed’ (past), and بن here functions as a nominal complement often meaning ‘root/foundation’ (literally ‘cast a thought into the root’ = ‘plant a thought’). The verb governs the object (اندیشه) and the locative/figurative noun بن.
segments.s602-g95: یکی خوب دستار بودش حریر is a descriptive nominal clause: یکی = ‘a certain/one’, خوب دستار = ‘a fine/beautiful turban’ (adjective precedes noun here for poetic effect), بودش = enclitic past ‘it was’, and حریر = ‘(made of) silk’. The copula + enclitic indicates existence/quality of the turban.
segments.s602-g96: به موزه درون پر ز مشک و عبیر describes the interior of the turban: به + موزه درون = ‘in the inner mūzeh/cap’ (به … درون = into/within), پر ز = ‘full of’ (پر = full, ز = poetic ‘of/from’), مشک و عبیر = ‘musk and fragrance/ambergris’. The construction lists the contents of the turban’s inner part.
segments.s602-g25: جهودیست is an archaic predicative phrase combining جهود (Jew) with the old copula -یست (is), so the clause reads ‘(the) dervish is a Jew.’ درویش is the noun (‘dervish/ascetic’) serving as the subject; the copula comes attached to the predicate, a common word order in poetic/classical Persian. و شب گرسنه is a coordinate phrase (‘and at night (he is) hungry’), where شب (night) is temporal and گرسنه (hungry) is a postpositive adjective describing his state.
segments.s602-g26: بخسپد is a classical/poetic verbal form meaning ‘he sleeps’ (the prefix ب- and the -د ending mark an old present/habitual form); همی is a habitual/continuous marker (‘continually/still’) that reinforces ongoing action. بر زمین برهنه is a prepositional phrase ‘on the bare ground,’ with بر (on) + زمین (ground) and the adjective برهنه (bare/naked) following the noun.
segments.s602-g27: بگفتند is the simple past plural ‘they said’ used in narrative; و بهرام گفت continues the narration with ‘and Bahram said.’ ار is the conditional marker ‘if,’ and سپنج is the noun ‘sponge’ (object of the conditional clause). Note that گفت here functions as a past-tense reporting verb.
segments.s602-g28: نیابم is a negative present/subjunctive form ‘if I do not find’ (ن- negative + یاب- root + -م 1st person ending), used in the protasis of the conditional. بدین خانه functions as a locative/adverbial ‘to/at this house’ (بدین = به + این). آیدت is a 3rd-person form with the clitic -ت ‘to you,’ here ‘will come to you’ or ‘befall you,’ and رنج is the noun ‘suffering/trouble’ that will befall.
segments.s602-g29: بدین در = ‘at this door’ (بدین = به + این, a demonstrative locative; در here is the noun ‘door’). بخسپم is the 1st-person form of the classical verb meaning ‘I will sleep / I sleep here’ (prefix ب- with -م 1st-person ending in poetic usage). نجویم is the negative subjunctive (ن- + جویم) meaning ‘I will not seek’; it governs سرای (‘house/dwelling’), the thing not sought.
segments.s602-g30: نخواهم is the negated first-person modal/auxiliary ‘I will not’ (ن- negation + خواهم ‘I will/want’). به چیزی دگر = ‘for anything else’ (به = to/for; چیزی = something/anything; دگر = other/else). کرد رای is a light-verb construction: کرد (‘do/make’) with رای (‘plan/opinion’) = ‘change/alter one’s plan/opinion’; together with نخواهم it means ‘I will not change my counsel/opinion for anything else.’
segments.s602-g31: به راهام گفت = ‘he said to Raham’ (به marks the indirect object). ای is the vocative particle ‘O!’. نبرده is a negative past-participial/attributive form (ن- + برده ‘taken’), used adjectivally to describe the rider as ‘not having taken’ (i.e., ‘un‑taken’/‘not-taken’); سوار is the noun ‘rider/horseman’ being vocatively addressed.
segments.s602-g32: همی is the habitual/continuous adverb ‘continually/still.’ رنجه here denotes ‘distress/trouble’ (object), and داری is the 2nd-person present of داشتن ‘to have’ used idiomatically with an object to mean ‘you cause/inflict (distress).’ م + را are the clitic pronoun + object marker (م = me, را = definite object marker) yielding مرا ‘me.’ خوارخوار is an emphatic adjective/adverb meaning ‘utterly contemptible/insulting,’ modifying how he treats the speaker.
segments.s602-g49: فرود آمد is a past-tense verbal phrase (literally 'fell/arrived down') meaning 'he alighted/dismounted.' The coordinating و links this action to the following clause; اسپ را is the definite direct object marked by را, and با + لگام gives the instrumental phrase 'with the bridle' (با as instrumental preposition). The main verb that governs اسپ را با لگام appears in the next segment.
segments.s602-g50: ببست is a past-tense verb 'he fastened/tied' (here used with the bridle). The coordinating و again links to the next verb phrase. برآهخت is an older/poetic past form meaning 'he raised/drew up' (here used with تیغ); the light preverb بر gives an upward/outward sense. از introduces the source 'from the scabbard' (نیام). Word order places the verb before its object and the source phrase.
segments.s602-g51: نمدزین is a compound noun (nمد + زین) meaning a felt placed on the saddle. بگسترد is an archaic past-form 'he spread' (ب- prefix plus root گستر). The coordinated phrase بالینش زین omits a copula: بالینش 'his pillow' (possessive -ش) and زین 'the saddle' are in apposition, i.e. 'the saddle served as his pillow.'
segments.s602-g52: بخفت is a past-tense verb meaning 'he lay down / slept.' The phrase و دو پایش کشان بر زمین is an adverbial participial description of manner: دو پایش ('his two feet' with the possessive suffix -ش) + کشان (a present-participle-like form 'dragging'), followed by the locative بر زمین 'on the ground.' The participle follows the noun it modifies, describing how he lay.
segments.s602-g53: جهود is a plural noun here (an archaic word meaning 'Jews' in classical Persian) functioning as the subject. آن is a demonstrative ('that/those') tied to جهود. در خانه 'in the house' is the locative, and از پس is a compound meaning 'from the rear/behind.' ببست is the past verb 'they shut/closed.' Word order is Subject – locative – directional phrase – verb, typical of older Persian narrative style.
segments.s602-g54: بیاورد is a past-tense 'he/they brought' and خوان means 'banquet/feast' as the object. The coordination و links to به خوردن نشست: به + verbal noun (خوردن) marks the purpose or activity 'to eating,' and نشست is the past 'sat' (i.e. 'sat down to eat'). This is a common classical Persian pattern: 'به + noun(verb) + نشست' = 'sat down to [verb].'
segments.s602-g55: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that' (ازان = 'from that'; پس = 'after/then'). به بهرام marks the indirect object ('to Bahram'), and گفت is the past verb 'he said' (here introducing direct speech). ای سوار is a vocative ('O rider'), placed after the reporting clause as the addressee; Persian often uses ای + noun for direct address.
segments.s602-g56: چو here means 'when' (or 'if') and takes a verb in the subjunctive/second-person form بشنوی 'you hear'—a temporal/conditional clause. یاد دار is an imperative-like instruction 'keep/hold in memory' (یاد = 'memory/remembrance', دار here functions as 'keep/have' in the sense 'remember'). The order is: temporal clause (چو این داستان بشنوی) followed by the command (یاد دار).
segments.s602-g33: This clause lists a hypothetical/undesirable event. بخسپی is an archaic/uncertain form here (treated as a nominal or particle introducing the condition); و is the coordinating 'and'; چیزت is 'your thing(s)' with the possessive -ت; بدزدد is a present/subjunctive/optative form meaning 'steals' (literary), and کسی is the indefinite 'someone'. Word order is literary: condition/event first, then the agent.
segments.s602-g34: ازان = 'from that / because of that' linking the result to the previous clause; رنجه here denotes causing distress or the state of distress (verbal/nominal use), داری is the present 'you have/you cause' (literary use of داشتن as a live/present-tense marker), م + را together mark the object 'me' (splitted in the line), تو is the emphatic/explicit 'you', and بسی = 'much/enough'. The sequence expresses result: 'because of that you give me much pain.'
segments.s602-g35: به خانه درآی is an imperative phrase: به + خانه ('into the house') + درآی (imperative 'enter/come in'). ار is an archaic conditional 'if'; جهان تنگ شد is a clause meaning 'the world/life becomes narrow' with شد as the past/become verb (here used as 'becomes'). The sequence gives a command followed by the conditional reason.
segments.s602-g36: همه کار is a noun phrase 'all affairs/all things'; بی‌برگ and بی‌رنگ are adjectival compounds with the negative prefix بی- ('without'), here poetically meaning 'barren/leafless' and 'colorless/dull'; و simply coordinates the two adjectives; شد is the past/become verb, yielding 'all affairs became barren and colorless.'
segments.s602-g37: به پیمان is an oath-like phrase 'by the covenant' or 'swear'; که introduces the content of the oath ('that'); چیزی نخواهی is second-person negative/future/optative meaning 'you will want nothing' or 'you will not ask for anything'; ز من is the poetic 'from me' ('of me'). The whole clause is a request/pledge formula.
segments.s602-g38: ندارم is the 1st-person present negative 'I do not have' or 'I have not'; به here marks the scope or is evaluative 'even/for' (literary usage) before مرگ (death); آبچین is a small water‑vessel/ewer associated with funerary rites and کفن is 'shroud'. The clause means 'I have nothing, not even a death-ewer or a shroud.'
segments.s602-g39: هم امشب sets temporal emphasis 'even/also tonight'; ترا here is the object 'you' (written as the clitic ت + را in the line); و نشست ترا = 'and your sitting/retinue/gathering' where نشست is used for a company/assembly and the second ت + را marks possession/object ('your ...'). The phrase names the beneficiaries who ought to be provided for tonight.
segments.s602-g40: خورش باید is an impersonal/assertive clause 'a stew/meat-dish ought to be (for you)' (باید = 'should/ought'); و connects the contrast; نیست چیزی مرا is an existential negative 'there is not anything for me' (نیست = 'is not', چیزی = 'anything/something', مرا = 'for me', here written as م + را). Together: 'There should be a meal, but I have nothing.'
segments.s602-g97: برون کرد و is a compound verbal phrase: برون (an adverb/prepositional element 'out,forth') precedes the past-tense verb کرد ('did/took'), so together 'took out'. The conjunction و links to the next action. سرگین بدو کرد has سرگین as the object 'dung', بدو as a dative 'to it/onto it', and کرد as a second past verb 'made/placed'—پاک at the end of this segment is an adverbial modifier ('entirely/cleanly') that attaches forward to the following verb phrase.
segments.s602-g98: بینداخت is a past tense verb ('threw, cast away') formed from the root انداخت with the prefix بی-; it carries the main action here. با خاک اندر مغاک is a prepositional/instrument phrase: با ('with/by') + خاک ('earth/soil') as the accompanying material, اندر ('into/within') + مغاک ('abyss') showing direction/place. Word order places the verb before its following prepositional phrases (poetic flexibility).
segments.s602-g99: به introduces an indirect object ('to/for'); راهام را is the recipient/target (a definite/known group) with را marking it as definite; گفت is the simple past 'said'. کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone, and پارسا is the vocative noun 'pious (one)'. The vocative phrase is inserted after the reporting verb and addresses the addressee directly.
segments.s602-g100: گر is the conditional 'if'. آزادیم is a predicate meaning 'we/one are free' (آزاد + -یم), and بشنود is a third-person present/subjunctive form of 'to hear' used here in the sense 'he hears' or optatively 'may he hear/should he hear'. پادشا is the subject 'the king'. Together the clause means 'if the king hears that we are free...' with a classical subjunctive nuance.
segments.s602-g101: ترا is the second-person object ('to you') formed from the 2nd-person suffix (ت) plus the object marker را; it appears before the rest for emphasis. از جهان is a prepositional phrase 'from the world' and بی‌نیازی is the noun 'freedom from need/independence' functioning as the thing given. دهد is a third-person present/subjunctive/optative of 'to give' often used in classical Persian to mean 'will grant' or 'may grant' here.
segments.s602-g102: بر introduces the beneficiary/target 'to/upon' and مهتران is the plural 'chiefs/elders' (here indicated by the poetic form), while سرفرازی is the direct object 'honor/glory' being given. دهد repeats the same third-person verb 'gives' to complete the parallel construction; the second clause mirrors the first in structure (prepositional target + object + verb).
segments.s602-g1: This is a temporal subordinate clause introduced by چو ('when'). ز پیش سواران is a prepositional phrase (ز + پیش + سواران) meaning 'from (out of) in front of the horsemen'; برگرفت is a simple past (3rd singular) 'took up / took' and is the clause's verb. The subject is omitted (typical in Persian poetry) and must be supplied from context.
segments.s602-g2: سوی introduces a direction 'toward(s)'; خان is the addressee/target ('khan' = lord/chief). بی‌بر is an epithet (here 'ill‑fated' or 'without fortune'). به + راهام is a directional phrase; in classical/poetic usage بدین/به + noun often compresses, and را here looks like the object/topic marker attached in an old orthography. تفت is a terse poetic verb describing swift motion (here 'sped/rode quickly') — the exact lexical senses listed do not match perfectly, so the gloss treats it as an archaic/poetic motion verb. Word order is inverted from modern prose but standard for Ferdowsi: direction + target + motion verb.
segments.s602-g3: بزد is a past tense verb 'he struck / knocked' and is directly followed by the noun در ('door') as its object (the verb + object order is verb–object here, common in poetry). بگفتا is a past-tense reporting verb 'he said'; که introduces the content/quotation clause that follows. بی‌شهریار is used vocatively or as an address 'O one without a king / kingless one'.
segments.s602-g4: بماندم is first-person past 'I remained / I stayed.' چو again introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when.' او is the 3rd-person pronoun 'he'; بازماند is a past/passive-ish form meaning 'was left behind / lagged' (here 'he lagged behind'); از شکار is a prepositional phrase 'from the hunt.' The clause structure is 'I stayed when he was left behind from the hunt.'
segments.s602-g5: شب آمد is a short clause 'night came' (simple past). ندانم is the negative present/imperfect first-person 'I do not know' (in classical Persian it can blur tense/aspect). همی here is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('indeed/at all') placed before the object; راه را is the definite object 'the way.' Word order (verb before object) is idiomatic in Ferdowsi.
segments.s602-g6: نیابم is a 1st-person form of یابیدن in a subjunctive/optative sense here — 'I may/ might find' or 'I will find' depending on nuance; following ندانم it yields 'I do not know whether I shall/might find...'. همی again is an emphatic/aspective particle. لشکر و شاه را is the coordinated definite object 'the army and the king' marked by را. So the clause reads 'I may not find the army and the king' or 'I do not know if I shall find the army and the king.'
segments.s602-g7: گر is the conditional 'if.' امشب is the adverb 'tonight.' بدین is a poetic contraction of به + این meaning 'to/at this' (so 'to this house'); it appears split in the tokens as ب + دین but functions syntactically as a single prepositional phrase 'to this.' یابم is the 1st-person subjunctive/optative 'I may find / I reach' (used in conditional protasis). سپنج here is a noun whose ordinary senses ('sponge') do not fit the context; it may be a proper name or a rare term in the poem — grammatically it follows the protasis and acts as a nominative or vocative/appendage.
segments.s602-g8: نباشد is a negative jussive/optative 'may there not be' or 'let there be no.' کسی را is the (indefinite) object 'anyone' (with را used in classical Persian after an indefinite when combined with negation to yield 'no one'); ز من is a prepositional phrase 'from me' or 'because of me'; هیچ رنج is the noun phrase 'any suffering / any harm.' Together the clause is a wish: 'may no one have any suffering from me.'
segments.s602-g73: که introduces a subordinate/relative clause ('that' / 'so that') linking this clause to what precedes. از جام is a prepositional phrase 'from the cup'; یابی is a classical subjunctive/optative (second‑person/indefinite) form of یابیدن meaning 'may you find/attain' and follows the noun phrase; سرانجام نیک is a noun phrase 'a good/outcome' with adjective نیک qualifying سرانجام.
segments.s602-g74: This is a coordinated list of offerings: خنک (an adjective 'cool/refreshing') qualifies میگسار (here an agent noun 'cup‑bearer, wine‑server'); و 'and' links the items; می‌و 'fruit' follows as another item; جام نیک is a noun + adjective 'a good/fine cup'. Note the poetic word order (adjective sometimes before the noun) and simple coordination by و.
segments.s602-g75: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; از کوه is the prepositional phrase 'from the mountain'; خنجر is the direct object 'dagger'; برآورد is the classical past verb 'brought out / drew' (poetic past) and comes after the object in this inverted poetic order; هور is the agent (proper name) placed at the end for emphasis.
segments.s602-g76: گریزان is a participial/adjectival form 'fleeing' and with the auxiliary شد 'became' forms an inchoative past meaning 'began to flee' or simply 'fled'; از خانه بهرام گور is the source phrase 'from the house of Bahram Gur' (از governs the whole noun phrase, with بهرام گور as the name).
segments.s602-g77: بران is a deictic/adverbial 'upon that' or 'thereon'; چرمه is the direct object 'hide/leather'; ناچران is an adverbial/adjectival 'reluctantly/compelled' modifying the action; زین نهاد is the verb phrase with نهاد (past tense 'placed/put') and زین ('saddle') marking where it was placed — overall: 'he, compelled, put the hide on that saddle.'
segments.s602-g78: چه here introduces an explanatory/casual clause ('for/indeed'), followed by زین 'that saddle'; از برش is a genitival/partitive phrase where برش should be read as 'its/his side/part' (poetic compression of بر + ـش), with از 'from/of'; خشک 'dry' modifies the saddle; بالین نهاد = 'placed at/by the bedside' with نهاد again the past 'placed' and بالین used adverbially 'by the pillow/bedside'. The clause explains why/how the saddle was set.
segments.s602-g79: بیامد is the past 'he came'; به introduces the destination/direction 'to/toward'; راهام is a compressed/poetic form involving راه (road) and the object/topic marker (here indicating direction or address) — functionally 'to the road' or 'to where the roads are'; گفت is the speech verb 'said' (poetic past) and is followed immediately by the vocative ای سوار 'O rider' which is the addressee.
segments.s602-g80: به گفتار خود is a prepositional phrase 'in/with your speech/word' (خود = 'your own' / 'self' used reflexively/emphatically); بر here has a distributive/adhesive sense 'hold to / stick to'; کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'; پای‌دار is an adjective 'steadfast/firm' used predicatively with an implied verb 'باش' (i.e. 'be steadfast now in your word').
segments.s602-g17: همی before the verb is an archaic/poetic adverb giving an ongoing or emphatic sense ('still/indeed'); از تو ('from you') is fronted, making تو the source/benefactor; من is the nominative 'I' placed before the object (خانه) for emphasis. خانه is the direct object placed before the finite verb خواهم (the first-person present/future auxiliary 'I will / I want'), so خواهم takes خانه as its object. سپنج is an archaic/poetic noun here functioning as a further specification of the requested lodging/shelter; its exact classical sense is rare/uncertain in modern Persian.
segments.s602-g18: نیارم is a negative/negated verb form here meaning 'I will not take/ask' (an archaic conjugation; the precise lemma in the annotation doesn't match modern paradigms). به + چیزت = 'of/for your thing(s)' (چیزت = your possessions). ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'from that (time) on / thereafter'. به رنج = 'to/in hardship' where به marks the state or result.
segments.s602-g19: چو + past form بشنید = a temporal clause 'when he heard'. پویان is a proper name here (Poyān) and is the subject of the main clause; بشد is an older/poetic past/copular verb meaning 'he became' or 'he took on the role', so پیشکار ('attendant') is a predicate-complement: 'he became an attendant / hurried on as an attendant.'
segments.s602-g20: به نزد = 'to / in the presence of'; the doubled prepositions (به نزد به ...) are stylistic and indicate movement toward someone. راهام in the text functions as the addressee (a proper name, 'Rahām') even though the lemma shown is the object marker را; گفت is the perfect/past verb 'said' (here used transitively) and introduces the reported content; این سوار is the direct object of the reported speech ('this rider...').
segments.s602-g21: همی again gives an emphatic/continuous flavor. ز ایدر = 'from here' (ز = from, ایدر = here). امشب 'tonight' is a temporal adverb. نخواهد + گذشت is a future/auxiliary negation forming 'will not pass' (نخواهد is the present-future auxiliary 'will', با negative construction here meaning 'will not').
segments.s602-g22: سخن گفتن is a verbal-nominal phrase 'speaking / to speak' (سخن = speech, گفتن = the infinitive/verbal noun 'to say'), و connects clauses; رای بسیار گشت is a causative/resultative clause: رای = 'counsel/opinion', بسیار = 'many/much', and گشت (past/epic perfect of گردیدن) = 'became/arose/occurred', so the clause means 'much counsel arose' or 'there was much discussion.'
segments.s602-g23: به راهام گفتش = 'he said to Rahām' (به + name = to someone; گفتش is the enclitic form 'he said to him'). که introduces the content clause. رو here is the imperative form of رفتن ('go') used colloquially/poetically as a command 'go!'; بی‌درنگ is an adverb 'without delay / immediately', modifying the imperative.
segments.s602-g24: بگویش is an imperative/hortative 'tell him' (بگو + ش enclitic = 'tell to him'); که introduces the subordinate content clause. این جایگاهیست تنگ is a nominal predicate: این = 'this', جایگاهیست is a poetic fused form 'is a place/position' (جایگاه + است contracted), and تنگ = 'narrow' — together, 'that this place is narrow.'
segments.s602-g9: به پیش (an adverbial phrase) = 'forward' or 'to the front'. به + راهام is a dative phrase 'to/unto (the) rāhâm' (here the addressee/attendant), then شد (past of شدن) is the motion verb 'came/stepped' and پیشکار is the subject 'the steward/attendant'. Word order is somewhat topical (adverbial/dative fronted) so literally 'forward, to the steward came the attendant.'
segments.s602-g10: بگفت is the simple past 'he said' introducing the reported content. آنچ is a relative/indefinite 'that which / what', and بشنید is past 'he heard' (so together آنچ بشنید = 'what he had heard'). ازان names the source 'from that/of that', and نامدار is an adjective 'renowned/illustrious' modifying the source. The whole clause is 'he said (بگفت) that which he had heard from that renowned one.'
segments.s602-g11: به راهام گفت = 'he said to the steward' (به + indirect object; گفت past 'said'). ایچ 'nothing/not at all' + ازین 'of/from this' + در here = 'in/at this matter' (a conventional phrase meaning 'about this') and مرنج is the negative imperative 'do not grieve / be upset.' So the line is an address: 'He said to the steward: Do not be at all upset about this.'
segments.s602-g12: بگویش is an imperative/jussive form 'tell him' (بگو + pronoun). که is the complementizer 'that', ایدر 'here', and نیابی is the negative subjunctive/optative form meaning 'you will not find / you will not get' (used here as reported content of the command). سپنج is the noun 'sponge.' Together: 'Tell him that he will not find a sponge here.'
segments.s602-g13: بیامد is the past 'came', فرستاده is 'the messenger', با او = 'with him' (or 'to him' depending on context) and بگفت is 'he said'. The sequence describes the arrival of a messenger who then spoke: 'A messenger came and said (to him)...'.
segments.s602-g14: که is the complementizer 'that'. ایدر 'here'. ت is the 2nd-person enclitic (addressing 'you'). را is the object/topic marker. نیست 'is not' and جای نهفت = 'a hiding-place' or 'place to hide' (جای = place; نهفت = hidden/concealed). The clause says 'that here there is not a place to hide you' i.e. 'there is no hiding-place for you here.'
segments.s602-g15: بدو is 'to him / unto him' (the dative), گفت is past 'said', بهرام is the speaker's name, با او 'with him' (or 'to him' depending on nuance), and بگوی is the jussive/imperative 'tell' — here Bahram commands the messenger: 'Tell him...' Note that classical word order allows these dative/indirect-object phrases to appear before the verb.
segments.s602-g16: کز = 'that / for that' introducing the content clause; ایدر = 'here'; گذشتن is the infinitive 'to pass / to go by'. مرا نیست روی is an idiomatic negative: مرا (to me / for me) + نیست 'is not' + روی 'face' — literally 'I have no face', idiomatically 'I will not / I have no inclination/courage to do so.' So the clause means 'that I will not pass by here' or 'that I have no intention of passing through here.'
segments.s602-g81: تو گفتی که = 'you said that' (تو = 2nd-singular pronoun; گفتی is the 2nd-singular past of گفتن used to introduce a reported clause). سرگین این بارگی is the noun phrase serving as the object/topic of the reported clause; note the poetic/inverted order (سرگین 'dung' comes before the demonstrative این) and بارگی here means a burden/load related to the dung.
segments.s602-g82: به جاروب روبم به یکبارگی = 'with a broom I sweep (it) in one go.' به جاروب is an instrumental prepositional phrase ('with a broom'); روبم is a 1st-person-form (subjunctive/optative) expressing the action 'that I sweep/clean' (here functioning inside the reported clause); به یکبارگی is an adverbial phrase 'all at once / suddenly' modifying the verb.
segments.s602-g83: کنون آنچ گفتی = 'now, that which you said' (کنون 'now' fronts the clause; آنچ = 'that which / whatever' and گفتی is the verb 'you said'). بروب و ببر are two coordinated imperatives/optative verbs: بروب 'sweep away' and ببر 'take away/carry off' (و links the two commands).
segments.s602-g84: به رنجم ز مهمان بیدادگر = a purpose/result phrase: به رنجم 'for my suffering / to my grief' (به + noun), ز = 'from / because of' expressing source, مهمان بیدادگر = 'the cruel/tyrannical guest' (بیدادگر qualifying مهمان). Together it gives the reason/purpose: so that I am not distressed by the cruel guest.
segments.s602-g85: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = to him/unto him; گفت = said). بهرام شو پایکار is an addressed command: 'Bahram, become (شو) پایکار' where پایکار = 'combatant/fighter' (an exhortation to assume the role of champion). The imperative شو follows the name in vocative order.
segments.s602-g86: بیاور که سرگین کشد بر کنار = 'bring (it), so that he may drag/carry the dung to the edge.' بیاور is the imperative 'bring'; که introduces the purpose/result clause; سرگین is the object 'dung'; کشد is a 3rd-person verb here meaning 'may pull/drag/carry' (subjunctive/optative sense after که), and بر کنار = 'to the bank/edge' (بر + کنار).
segments.s602-g87: دهم زر = 'I will give ten (pieces of) gold' — دهم is an archaic/poetic 1st-person form meaning 'I give / I will give' (optative/future sense). که تا introduces purpose: که + تا خاک بیرون برد = 'so that he may take the earth/dirt out.' تا here reinforces the purposive clause ('so that / in order that'), خاک بیرون = 'the dirt outside', and برد is the verb 'carry/take' in its 3rd-person form.
segments.s602-g88: وزین خانه تو به هامون برد = 'and (he) carries your heavy house to the plain.' وزین is an adjective 'weighty/heavy' modifying خانه 'house'; تو = 'your'; به هامون = 'to the plain'; برد again is the 3rd-person verb 'he will carry/take.' The adjective–noun order is slightly elevated/poetic.
segments.s602-g65: خروشید is a past/perfective verb meaning 'cried out/roared' (3rd person in narrative); it introduces speech. کای is a vocative particle 'O' addressing someone directly. رنج‌دیده is a compound adjective 'suffering, distressed' (literally 'having suffered') modifying the following noun. سوار is the noun 'rider/horseman' and completes the vocative phrase 'کای رنج‌دیده سوار' = 'O suffering rider'.
segments.s602-g66: برین is the fused preposition+demonstrative 'بر این' meaning 'to/for this' (pointing to the tale). داستان کهن is a noun phrase 'ancient tale' (داستان = tale/legend, کهن = ancient). گوش‌دار here is an exhortative/imperative vocative meaning 'give ear! / listen!' directed at the addressee — it functions as the command that follows the vocative.
segments.s602-g67: The clause begins with the relativizer/connector که and the universal هرکس که = 'whoever'. دارد is the present tense verb 'has/possesses'. دلش is the possessed noun 'his heart' (دل + possessive -ش). روشنست is a predicative adjective + copula 'is bright/clear' (روشن + -ست = 'is clear'), so the whole means 'whoever whose heart is clear'.
segments.s602-g68: درم پیش او is a prepositional phrase 'a dirham before him' (درم = coin, پیش = in front of/in the presence of, او = him). چون introduces a simile 'like/as'. یکی جوشنست is an equative clause 'is like one cuirass' (یکی = one, جوشنست = 'is a cuirass' — جوشن + -ست as copula), so the comparison reads 'a dirham before him is as (good as) a single cuirass.'
segments.s602-g69: کسی 'someone/anyone' begins the negative/existential construction. کو here is an archaic relative particle used like 'who' (classical usage; not the modern interrogative 'where'). ندارد بود is an older compound verbal sequence: ندارد ('has' or 'does not have', literal stem) plus the past auxiliary بود, producing a past/relative sense 'there was (no one) who had / who did not have' in context; the combination yields 'there was no one who had' or idiomatically 'there was no one whose'. خشک لب is a compound adjective 'dry-lipped' (خشک = dry, لب = lip), describing that person's condition.
segments.s602-g70: چنانچون is a comparative 'just as/like'. توی here is ambiguous: in classical/poetic usage it can mark 'in/at' or function as an old form related to 'تو' ('you'); contextually it reads as 'like you (being) hungry at midnight' or 'as in the hungry midnight.' گرسنه is the adjective 'hungry' and نیم‌شب is the temporal phrase 'midnight'. The whole phrase gives the simile 'like you, hungry at midnight.'
segments.s602-g71: بدو is the dative clitic 'to him' (introducing the indirect object). گفت here is the past tense 'said' (classical narrative past). بهرام is the speaker's name. کاین is the demonstrative 'that this' introducing the content of his speech. بس is an intensifier here (classical use = 'very'), and شگفت is the adjective 'wondrous/strange' — together 'بس شگفت' = 'very wondrous/so strange.'
segments.s602-g72: به گیتی is a locative phrase 'in/throughout the world' (به = in/to, گیتی = world). مرین is uncertain/archaic here; as printed it resembles an imperative or verbal form related to 'مرگ/مر' (dying) but in context functions with یاد to mean 'the remembrance of dying' or more broadly 'remembrance/memory (that concerns mortality)'; its exact morphological analysis is irregular and is marked accordingly. یاد 'remembrance/memory' is the noun. باید is the modal 'must/ought' and گرفت (past infinitival form of گرفتن) here means 'to be taken/learned' — together باید گرفت = 'must be taken/learned/acquired.' The clause therefore reads roughly 'in the world one must acquire/learn remembrance (of mortality).'

sentences.s602-s21: If this steed were to cast dung and water, or if it were to break a brick of this house.
sentences.s602-s22: At night you should take out its dung and sweep it away, and carry its dust off to the plain.
sentences.s602-s23: You shall also pay compensation for that same brick; when you wake from sleep, give it then.
sentences.s602-s24: To him Bahram said, 'I will make a pact; I will set my head as hostage for these hardships.'
sentences.s602-s29: In this world, whoever is afflicted should not look down on the needy among people.
sentences.s602-s30: Bahram said to him: I have heard this tale from ancient reports.
sentences.s602-s31: I heard it by report, and now I see that you have read it from the guiding account.
sentences.s602-s32: He relates that when the Jew's bread was eaten, from that the rejoicing increased.
sentences.s602-s45: He said to him, “I have no one who will pick up the earth and carry it off and pour it into the abyss.”
sentences.s602-s46: You made a covenant: do not commit crookedness; I ought not to call you a tyrant.
sentences.s602-s47: When Bahram heard this speech from him, he cast (or planted) a fresh thought to its root.
sentences.s602-s48: There was a certain fine turban, it was of silk; its inner cap was full of musk and fragrance.
sentences.s602-s13: The dervish is a Jew; and at night, hungry, he sleeps on the bare ground.
sentences.s602-s14: They said, and Bahram said: 'If I do not find a sponge, trouble will come to this house.'
sentences.s602-s15: I will sleep at this very door; I will not seek a house, nor will I change my counsel for anything else.
sentences.s602-s16: He said to Raham, 'O rider who has not taken (it), you continually cause me distress and humiliate me utterly.'
sentences.s602-s25: He dismounted, fastened the horse with the bridle, and drew his sword from the scabbard.
sentences.s602-s26: He spread a felt over the saddle; the saddle was his pillow, and he lay down, his two feet dragging on the ground.
sentences.s602-s27: Those Jews shut the back door of the house, brought in the banquet, and sat down to eat.
sentences.s602-s28: After that he said to Bahram, 'O rider, when you hear this story, remember it.'
sentences.s602-s17: Even if someone steals your things, you cause me great distress because of that.
sentences.s602-s18: Come into the house; if the world becomes narrow, all affairs will become leafless and colorless.
sentences.s602-s19: Swear by the covenant that you will want nothing from me; I have nothing — not even a death-ewer or a shroud.
sentences.s602-s20: Even tonight you and your company should have a meal — and I have nothing.
sentences.s602-s49: He took it out, poured dung upon it, and threw it, with earth, into the abyss.
sentences.s602-s50: He said to the wayfarers, 'O pious one, if the king hears that we are free...'
sentences.s602-s51: He will grant you freedom from the world and give you honor among the chiefs.
sentences.s602-s1: When he took the road from ahead of the horsemen, he rode swiftly toward the ill-fated khan.
sentences.s602-s2: He struck the door and said, 'O one without a sovereign, I stayed when he was left behind from the hunt.'
sentences.s602-s3: Night came; I do not know the way, and I may not find the army and the king.
sentences.s602-s4: If tonight I find this house, may no one suffer any harm because of me.
sentences.s602-s37: That from the cup you may attain a good outcome — a cool cup-bearer, fruit, and a fine cup.
sentences.s602-s38: When Hūr drew a dagger from the mountain, he fled from the house of Bahram Gur.
sentences.s602-s39: He reluctantly placed the hide on that saddle; for he had set that dry saddle beside his bed.
sentences.s602-s40: He came to the road and said, 'O rider, be steadfast now in your word.'
sentences.s602-s9: I will ask you for a house for shelter; I will not take any of your possessions — after that (I shall be) in hardship.
sentences.s602-s10: When Poyān heard, he hurried forward as an attendant and went to Rahām and said, 'This rider...'.
sentences.s602-s11: Indeed, he will not pass from here tonight; many words were spoken and much counsel was held.
sentences.s602-s12: He told Rahām, 'Go at once and tell him that this place is narrow.'
sentences.s602-s5: The steward stepped forward and said what he had heard from that renowned man.
sentences.s602-s6: He said to the steward, 'Do not be at all troubled about this matter' — and added, 'Tell him that you will not find a sponge here.'
sentences.s602-s7: A messenger came and, with him, said: 'There is no place here in which you can hide.'
sentences.s602-s8: Bahram said to him, 'Tell (them) that I will not pass by here.'
sentences.s602-s41: You said that I should sweep this dung away with a broom all at once.
sentences.s602-s42: Now, do what you said—sweep it away and carry it off—so that I not be afflicted by the cruelty of the guest.
sentences.s602-s43: He said to him, 'Bahram, be the fighter; bring it, so that he may carry the dung to the edge.'
sentences.s602-s44: I'll give ten pieces of gold so that he removes the dirt outside and carries your heavy house to the plain.
sentences.s602-s33: He cried out, “O suffering rider, give ear to this ancient tale!”
sentences.s602-s34: For whoever has a clear heart, a dirham before him is like a single cuirass.
sentences.s602-s35: There was no one whose lips were dry like yours, hungry at midnight.
sentences.s602-s36: To him Bahram said, “This is very wondrous; in the world one must acquire/learn remembrance.”
